Validate supervision gates and protected-pattern enforcement end-to-end with detailed JSONL logging.

- [ ] Protected patterns always block kill actions.
- [ ] Failure paths produce clear errors + correct exit codes.
- [ ] JSONL logs + artifact manifests validate.

- E2E script via shared harness and fixtures from bd-3gkt/bd-z3ma.

- [ ] Error paths produce correct exit codes and guidance.
- [ ] JSONL logs + artifact manifests validate.

## Test Plan
- E2E script using shared harness + fixtures: run `pt agent plan/apply --dry-run` against crafted inputs.

 {"id":"bd-1zkm","title":"Final CI verification: build, test, clippy, format","description":"# Final CI Verification

## What
Complete verification that the ftui migration is production-ready. Run the full CI
pipeline locally and fix any remaining issues. This is the final gate before the
migration can be considered complete.

This bead depends on ALL implementation and test beads being complete:
- bd-342l: ratatui/crossterm removed
- bd-1ujr: 68 Rust E2E workflow tests pass
- bd-2jlf: BATS E2E TUI tests pass
- bd-hak3: 68+ unit tests pass
- bd-36a2: golden/snapshot + integration tests pass

---

## Verification Steps

### 1. Build (all feature combinations)
```bash
cargo build -p pt-core                    # without ui feature
cargo build -p pt-core --features ui      # with ftui
cargo build -p pt-core --release --features ui  # release build
cargo check -p pt-core --all-features     # all features together
```

### 2. Tests (full suite)
```bash
# Unit tests (68+ component tests)
cargo test -p pt-core --features ui

# Golden/snapshot tests
cargo test --test tui_golden -p pt-core --features ui

# Integration tests
cargo test --test tui_integration -p pt-core --features ui

# Rust E2E workflow tests (68 tests)
cargo test --test tui_e2e_workflows -p pt-core --features ui

# BATS E2E tests (68 existing + 8 new ftui-specific)
bats test/test_e2e_tui.bats
```

### 3. Clippy (zero warnings)
```bash
cargo clippy -p pt-core --features ui -- -D warnings
```

### 4. Format (clean)
```bash
cargo fmt -p pt-core -- --check
```

### 5. No ratatui/crossterm remnants
```bash
# Verify no ratatui imports remain
rg "use ratatui" crates/pt-core/src/
rg "use crossterm" crates/pt-core/src/
# Both should return nothing
```

### 6. Feature gate works
```bash
# Without ui feature, TUI code should be excluded
cargo build -p pt-core 2>&1 | grep -c "ftui" # should be 0
```

### 7. JSONL Logging Verification
```bash
# Launch TUI briefly, verify JSONL log is created and valid
timeout 3 cargo run -p pt-core --features ui -- run --interactive --standalone 2>/dev/null || true
# Check last session log file
jq . ~/.config/process_triage/tui_*.jsonl | head -20
# Verify structured events present: tui.startup, tui.shutdown
```

### 8. Manual Smoke Test
```bash
cargo run -p pt-core --release --features ui -- run --interactive --standalone
# Verify: TUI launches, processes display, navigation works, quit works
# Test: theme switching, search, detail views, help overlay
```

---

## Performance Targets (from Premium TUI spec)

These targets MUST be verified during the manual smoke test. ftui's deterministic
rendering and Bayesian diff strategy should make these easy to hit, but they must be
confirmed:

| Metric | Target | How to Measure |
|--------|--------|----------------|
| Frame render time | < 16ms (60fps capable) | `tui.render_frame` events in JSONL log |
| Input latency | < 50ms keypress → visual feedback | Subjective + JSONL timestamp diff |
| Memory overhead | < 10MB TUI vs CLI mode | `ps aux` comparison |
| Startup to first render | < 200ms | `tui.startup` → first `tui.render_frame` delta |
| Exit cleanup | < 50ms | `tui.shutdown` timestamp vs process exit |

### Automated Performance Check
```bash
# Extract render times from JSONL log
jq -r 'select(.fields.message == "tui.render_frame") | .fields.duration_us' \
  ~/.config/process_triage/tui_*.jsonl | \
  awk '{sum+=$1; count++} END {print "avg_render_us=" sum/count " max=" max " count=" count}'
# avg should be well under 16000 (16ms)
```

---

## Known Risk Areas
- ftui nightly requirement vs process_triage edition
- Send + 'static bounds on async closures in Cmd::perform
- Widget rendering differences between ratatui and ftui (pixel-level)
- Terminal cleanup on panic paths
- JSONL log file rotation (don't let it grow unbounded)

## Files to Review
- All `tui/` source files — no ratatui imports
- All `tests/tui_*.rs` files — all pass
- `Cargo.toml` — ftui in dependencies, no ratatui/crossterm
- `rust-toolchain.toml` if modified
- JSONL log files — valid structured events

## Acceptance Criteria
- All builds succeed (debug + release, with and without ui feature)
- All tests pass: unit (68+), golden/snapshot (12+), integration (15+), E2E Rust (68), BATS (76+)
- Total test count: 229+ tests covering TUI code
- Clippy clean (no warnings with -D warnings)
- Format clean (rustfmt check passes)
- No ratatui/crossterm imports remain in source code
- Feature gate: `cargo build` without ui feature excludes TUI code
- JSONL structured logging produces valid events
- Performance targets met: <16ms render, <50ms input latency, <10MB overhead, <200ms startup

 {"id":"bd-20wt","title":"Consolidate agent show/status commands into sessions","description":"## Task
Consolidate `agent show` and `agent status` into the existing `agent sessions` command.

## Background
The CLI specification defines:
- `agent show --session <id>` - Show session details
- `agent status --session <id>` - Show session status

The current implementation has:
- `agent sessions` - List sessions

These commands have overlapping functionality. Consolidating into sessions:
- Reduces command surface complexity
- Makes discovery easier (one command for all session operations)
- Aligns with "sessions" being the noun for session-related queries

## Decision: Consolidate into sessions

### Current sessions command
```
pt agent sessions [OPTIONS]
  --limit <N>       Limit results
  --state <state>   Filter by state
  --cleanup         Remove old sessions
  --older-than <d>  Age threshold for cleanup
```

### Enhanced sessions command
```
pt agent sessions [OPTIONS]
  --session <id>    Show details for specific session
  --detail          Include full detail (plan contents, actions, evidence)
  --limit <N>       Limit results when listing
  --state <state>   Filter by state when listing
  --cleanup         Remove old sessions
  --older-than <d>  Age threshold for cleanup
```

## Behavior Matrix

| Flags | Behavior |
|-------|----------|
| (none) | List all sessions, summary view |
| `--session <id>` | Show session status + basic info |
| `--session <id> --detail` | Show session with full details (plan, actions, evidence) |
| `--limit N` | List up to N sessions |
| `--state X` | List sessions in state X |
| `--cleanup` | Delete old sessions |
| `--session X --cleanup` | **ERROR**: Cannot combine --session with --cleanup |

### Flag Conflict Handling
The following combinations are **invalid** and should return an error:
- `--session <id> --cleanup` - Cannot target specific session AND cleanup (use --older-than instead)
- `--session <id> --limit N` - Limit only applies to list mode
- `--session <id> --state X` - State filter only applies to list mode

Error message example:
```json
{
  "error": "invalid_flag_combination",
  "message": "--session cannot be combined with --cleanup. To cleanup, use --cleanup --older-than <duration>",
  "conflicting_flags": ["--session", "--cleanup"]
}
```

### Output: List Mode (no --session)
```json
{
  "sessions": [
    {
      "id": "pt-20260122-140000-a1b2",
      "state": "completed",
      "created": "2026-01-22T14:00:00Z",
      "candidate_count": 5,
      "actions_taken": 3
    }
  ],
  "total": 12,
  "showing": 10
}
```

### Output: Session Status (--session, no --detail)
```json
{
  "session_id": "pt-20260122-140000-a1b2",
  "state": "completed",
  "created": "2026-01-22T14:00:00Z",
  "updated": "2026-01-22T14:05:00Z",
  "candidate_count": 5,
  "actions": {
    "planned": 5,
    "executed": 3,
    "verified": 3
  },
  "outcome_summary": "3 processes terminated, 2.1GB freed"
}
```

### Output: Session Detail (--session --detail)
```json
{
  "session_id": "pt-20260122-140000-a1b2",
  "state": "completed",
  "created": "2026-01-22T14:00:00Z",
  "updated": "2026-01-22T14:05:00Z",
  "manifest": { ... },
  "plan": {
    "candidates": [ ... ],
    "generated_at": "..."
  },
  "actions": {
    "applied": [ ... ],
    "verifications": [ ... ]
  },
  "evidence": {
    "snapshots": [ ... ]
  }
}
```

## Implementation

### AgentSessionsArgs struct
```rust
#[derive(Args, Debug)]
struct AgentSessionsArgs {
    /// Show details for specific session (consolidates show/status)
    #[arg(long, alias = "status", alias = "show")]
    session: Option<String>,
    
    /// Include full session detail (plan, actions, evidence)
    #[arg(long)]
    detail: bool,
    
    /// Maximum sessions to return in list mode
    #[arg(long, default_value = "10")]
    limit: u32,
    
    /// Filter by session state (planning, applying, completed, failed)
    #[arg(long)]
    state: Option<String>,
    
    /// Remove old sessions
    #[arg(long)]
    cleanup: bool,
    
    /// Sessions older than this are removed (e.g., "7d", "24h")
    #[arg(long, default_value = "7d")]
    older_than: String,
}
```

### Validation Logic
```rust
fn validate_sessions_args(args: &AgentSessionsArgs) -> Result<(), Error> {
    if args.session.is_some() && args.cleanup {
        return Err(Error::InvalidFlagCombination {
            flags: vec!["--session", "--cleanup"],
            message: "--session cannot be combined with --cleanup",
        });
    }
    if args.session.is_some() && args.limit != 10 {
        return Err(Error::InvalidFlagCombination {
            flags: vec!["--session", "--limit"],
            message: "--limit only applies to list mode (without --session)",
        });
    }
    if args.session.is_some() && args.state.is_some() {
        return Err(Error::InvalidFlagCombination {
            flags: vec!["--session", "--state"],
            message: "--state only applies to list mode (without --session)",
        });
    }
    Ok(())
}
```

### Valid State Values
- `planning` - Session created, plan being generated
- `planned` - Plan ready, not yet applied
- `applying` - Actions being executed
- `verifying` - Verification in progress
- `completed` - All actions verified
- `failed` - Error occurred
- `cancelled` - User cancelled

### Deprecation Strategy
- `agent show` and `agent status` commands NOT removed (breaking change)
- Instead: `--status` and `--show` are hidden aliases for `--session`
- Help text clarifies: "Use `--session <id>` for session details"

### Issue 1: Missing IdentityQuality::Moderate variant
- File: crates/pt-core/src/collect/macos.rs, line 720
- Uses IdentityQuality::Moderate which does not exist in the enum
- Enum at crates/pt-common/src/id.rs:143 has only: Full, NoBootId, PidOnly
- Fix: Change to IdentityQuality::NoBootId (macOS has PID + start_time but no boot_id)

### Issue 2: Unconditional systemd imports in prechecks.rs
- File: crates/pt-core/src/action/prechecks.rs, line 14
- use crate::collect::systemd::{collect_systemd_unit, SystemdUnit, SystemdUnitType}; is unconditional
- The systemd module is cfg-gated to Linux in collect/mod.rs line 43
- Fix: Add #[cfg(target_os = "linux")] to line 14
- Complication: SupervisorInfo struct (lines 82-96) uses SystemdUnit and SystemdUnitType in its fields
- SupervisorInfo and its from_systemd_unit() method also need cfg-gating, OR the systemd types need platform-independent stubs
- Recommended approach: cfg-gate the SupervisorInfo fields that reference systemd types, and cfg-gate from_systemd_unit()

### Issue 3: Unconditional cgroup/container imports in container_supervision.rs
- File: crates/pt-core/src/supervision/container_supervision.rs, lines 20-24
- Imports from crate::collect::cgroup and crate::collect::container unconditionally
- Both modules are #[cfg(target_os = "linux")] in collect/mod.rs lines 26-28
- The module itself is not cfg-gated in supervision/mod.rs line 48
- Public re-exports at supervision/mod.rs lines 70-74 are also unconditional
- Fix: Either cfg-gate the entire container_supervision module and its re-exports, OR add conditional imports with stub implementations for non-Linux
- Recommended: cfg-gate the entire module (#[cfg(target_os = "linux")] mod container_supervision;) since container supervision is fundamentally Linux-specific

### Issue 4: SupervisorInfo struct cross-platform compatibility
- The SupervisorInfo struct uses SystemdUnit and SystemdUnitType in its fields
- These types come from the Linux-only systemd module
- Options: (a) Make struct fields Option<> with cfg attributes, (b) cfg-gate the struct, (c) move types to pt-common
- Recommended: Use cfg attributes on individual fields since SupervisorInfo is used cross-platform for non-systemd supervisors too

### Issue 5: No release ever cut
- Release workflow exists at .github/workflows/release.yml with 6 targets including macOS
- Triggered by v* tag push
- After fixing compilation, push a v0.1.0 tag (or whatever version matches VERSION file)
- Verify VERSION file, pt script version, and tag version all match (workflow validates this)

2. cfg-gate systemd imports in prechecks.rs (2-3 lines + struct field attrs)
3. cfg-gate container_supervision module in supervision/mod.rs (3-4 lines)
4. Cross-compile check: cargo check --target aarch64-apple-darwin (if toolchain available) or verify via CI
5. Run cargo check --workspace on Linux to confirm no regressions
6. Run cargo test on Linux to confirm no test regressions
7. Push, trigger CI, verify macOS builds pass
8. Tag and release

- crates/pt-core/src/supervision/mod.rs (line 48: cfg-gate container_supervision; lines 70-74: cfg-gate re-exports)
- crates/pt-core/src/supervision/container_supervision.rs (verify internal cfg gates are sufficient once module itself is gated)

 {"id":"bd-2yz0","title":"Unify threshold naming with --min-posterior alias","description":"## Task
Add `--min-posterior` as the canonical name for the posterior threshold flag, with `--threshold` as a backward-compatible alias.

## Background
The CLI specification uses `--min-posterior` while some implementations use `--threshold`. Both terms refer to the minimum posterior probability threshold for candidate selection.

For consistency with:
- Bayesian terminology (posterior probability)
- `agent apply --min-posterior` (if it exists)
- Academic/statistical convention

We should make `--min-posterior` the canonical name.

## Implementation

### Current (inconsistent)
```rust
/// Minimum posterior threshold
#[arg(long, default_value = "0.7")]
threshold: f64,
```

### Updated (canonical + alias)
```rust
/// Minimum posterior probability threshold for candidate selection
#[arg(long = "min-posterior", visible_alias = "threshold", default_value = "0.7")]
min_posterior: f64,
```

### Help Text Display
```
--min-posterior <VALUE>  Minimum posterior probability threshold [default: 0.7]
                         [aliases: threshold]
```

## Commands to Update
1. `agent plan` - Primary target (DONE if implemented)
2. `agent apply` - Verify consistency
3. Any other commands using threshold terminology

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] `--min-posterior` is the primary flag name in help
- [ ] `--threshold` continues to work (backward compatibility)
- [ ] Both flags set the same internal value
- [ ] Help text shows alias relationship
- [ ] No breaking changes for existing scripts using --threshold
- [ ] Variable name in code is `min_posterior` (not `threshold`)

## Testing Requirements

### Unit Tests
```rust

    // Help shows --min-posterior as primary
}

}

    // Default threshold is 0.7
}

#[test]

    // Using both --min-posterior and --threshold is either:
    // - An error, or
    // - Last one wins (clap default)
    // Document expected behavior
}
```

### Integration Tests

    // pt agent plan --min-posterior 0.9
    // Verify threshold is applied correctly
}

#[test]

    // pt agent plan --threshold 0.9
    // Same behavior as --min-posterior
}

    # Non-zero is OK if it is just "no candidates" - check output
    if grep -q "error" /tmp/output.json 2>/dev/null; then
        log "✗ --min-posterior flag not recognized"
        cat /tmp/output.json >> "$LOG"
        exit 1
    fi
    log "✓ --min-posterior flag accepted (exit $EXIT_CODE, likely no candidates)"
fi

# Test --threshold alias still works
log "Testing --threshold alias..."
if pt-core agent plan --threshold 0.85 --format json > /tmp/output.json 2>&1; then
    log "✓ --threshold alias works (exit 0)"
else
    EXIT_CODE=$?
    if grep -q "error" /tmp/output.json 2>/dev/null; then
        log "✗ --threshold alias failed"
        cat /tmp/output.json >> "$LOG"
        exit 1
    fi
    log "✓ --threshold alias accepted (exit $EXIT_CODE)"
fi

# Test default value is 0.7
log "Testing default value..."
OUTPUT=$(pt-core agent plan --format json 2>&1 || true)
# Check if threshold_used or equivalent field shows 0.7
if echo "$OUTPUT" | jq -e ".threshold_used == 0.7 or .min_posterior == 0.7" > /dev/null 2>&1; then
    log "✓ Default threshold is 0.7"
else
    log "⚠ Could not verify default (field may have different name or not in output)"
fi

# Test help shows --min-posterior as primary
log "Testing help output..."
HELP=$(pt-core agent plan --help 2>&1)
if echo "$HELP" | grep -q "min-posterior"; then
    log "✓ Help shows --min-posterior"
else
    log "✗ Help should show --min-posterior"
    exit 1
fi

# Test help shows threshold as alias
if echo "$HELP" | grep -qi "threshold"; then
    log "✓ Help mentions threshold (as alias)"
else
    log "⚠ Help might not show alias (acceptable if alias works)"
fi

# Test both flags produce same results (structure, not values that vary)
log "Testing equivalence..."
OUTPUT1=$(pt-core agent plan --min-posterior 0.8 --format json 2>&1 | jq -S "del(.session_id, .timestamp, .generated_at)" 2>/dev/null || echo "{}")
OUTPUT2=$(pt-core agent plan --threshold 0.8 --format json 2>&1 | jq -S "del(.session_id, .timestamp, .generated_at)" 2>/dev/null || echo "{}")

# Compare structure (fields present), not exact values
FIELDS1=$(echo "$OUTPUT1" | jq -r "keys | sort | .[]" 2>/dev/null || echo "")
FIELDS2=$(echo "$OUTPUT2" | jq -r "keys | sort | .[]" 2>/dev/null || echo "")

if [ "$FIELDS1" = "$FIELDS2" ]; then
    log "✓ Both flags produce same output structure"
else
    log "⚠ Output structure differs (may be due to timing/candidates)"
fi

log "=== E2E Test PASSED ==="

 {"id":"bd-35oy","title":"Fix inference test failures: default priors missing command_categories","description":"## Summary
Two tests in interpretation_examples.rs are failing because the default priors don't include command_categories Dirichlet distributions:

1. example_1_bun_test_stalled_signals_shift_posterior - Got abandoned=0.000 vs useful=0.051
2. example_4_claude_process_stalled - Got abandoned=0.367 (expected >0.5)

## Root Cause
The zombie class has extreme priors for boolean features (orphan_beta, tty_beta, net_beta, io_active_beta) that are absorbing probability mass that should go to abandoned. Without command_categories, the model cannot distinguish between abandoned agent and actual zombie process.

## Evidence
- priors.default.json has no command_categories section
- Tests pass command_category evidence (CommandCategory::Agent, CommandCategory::Test)
- log_lik_dirichlet returns 0.0 when command_categories is None

## Proposed Fix
Add command_categories to priors.default.json with Dirichlet alphas that disfavor zombie class for Agent/Test/Server categories.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-01-22T08:05:14.684642120Z","created_by":"TealGorge","updated_at":"2026-01-22T08:12:21.200658743Z","closed_at":"2026-01-22T08:12:21.200606324Z","close_reason":"fixed","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
 {"id":"bd-36a2","title":"Port TUI golden, snapshot, and integration tests to ftui-harness","description":"# Port TUI Golden, Snapshot, and Integration Tests to ftui-harness

## What
Migrate **both** the TUI golden tests (crates/pt-core/tests/tui_golden.rs, ~100 lines) **and** the TUI integration tests (crates/pt-core/tests/tui_integration.rs, ~83 lines) from ratatui TestBackend/Buffer assertions to ftui-harness snapshot testing and ftui Model-based assertions.

This bead consolidates what were previously two separate test beads (golden tests + integration tests) because both share test infrastructure, both depend on the same widget implementations being complete, and combining them enables better test organization: golden/snapshot tests validate visual output, integration tests validate state transitions and interaction flows.

---

## Part A: Golden/Snapshot Tests (formerly standalone bead)

### Current Golden Tests
- Render ProcessDetail widget with sample data → assert buffer contents
- Render ProcessTable with sample rows → assert column layout
- Render Galaxy Brain view → assert mathematical output format
- Uses ratatui::backend::TestBackend with fixed dimensions

### ftui-harness Snapshot Framework
ftui provides `ftui-harness` crate with:
- `TestTerminal` — in-memory terminal with buffer capture
- Snapshot comparison with ANSI output
- `BLESS=1` to update baselines
- Deterministic rendering (same input = same output, guaranteed by ftui proofs)

### Usage Pattern
```rust
use ftui_harness::TestTerminal;

#[test]
fn process_table_renders_correctly() {
    let mut term = TestTerminal::new(120, 40);
    let model = create_test_model_with_rows();
    term.render(|frame| model.view(frame));
    term.assert_snapshot("process_table_default");
}
```

### Migration Strategy
1. Replace ratatui TestBackend with ftui-harness TestTerminal
2. Create sample data fixtures (ProcessRow instances with known values)
3. Render Model::view() into TestTerminal
4. Generate initial baseline snapshots with `BLESS=1 cargo test`
5. Future test runs compare against baselines

### Snapshot Test Cases (~12)
- ProcessTable default layout (120x40)
- ProcessTable compact layout (80x24)
- ProcessDetail Summary view with classification badge
- ProcessDetail Genealogy tree view
- ProcessDetail Galaxy Brain mathematical trace
- SearchInput with active query
- ConfirmDialog with buttons
- Help overlay
- StatusBar with scan info
- ConfigEditor form fields
- Dark theme rendering
- High contrast theme rendering

### Snapshot Files
Store snapshots in `crates/pt-core/tests/snapshots/`. ftui-harness stores them as `.snapshot` files with ANSI content.

---

## Part B: Integration Tests (absorbed from bd-1gut)

### Current Integration Tests
- Galaxy brain split view rendering
- Help overlay rendering
- App-level state transitions
- Uses ratatui::Terminal<TestBackend> for rendering

### ftui Model Integration Test Pattern
In ftui, integration tests can:
1. Create a Model instance
2. Send Messages via update()
3. Assert state changes
4. Render into TestTerminal and assert visual output

```rust
#[test]
fn help_overlay_shows_on_question_mark() {
    let mut app = create_test_app();
    assert_eq\!(app.state, AppState::Normal);
    
    app.update(Msg::ToggleHelp);
    assert_eq\!(app.state, AppState::Help);
    
    let mut term = TestTerminal::new(120, 40);
    term.render(|frame| app.view(frame));
    // Assert help content is visible in buffer
}
```

### Integration Test Cases (~15)
- Help overlay toggle: Normal → Help → Normal
- Search mode transitions: Normal → Searching → Normal
- Confirm dialog lifecycle: Normal → Confirming → Execute/Cancel
- Detail pane toggle shows/hides
- Detail view switching (Summary → Genealogy → Galaxy Brain)
- Theme switching preserves layout structure
- Responsive layout at 4 breakpoints (Wide/Standard/Compact/Minimal)
- Selection state persists across filter changes
- Sort toggle reverses order
- Refresh message replaces rows without losing selection
- Focus cycling via Tab/Shift+Tab
- Execute flow: select → confirm → execute → outcome display
- Empty process list renders gracefully
- Terminal too small shows minimal view or warning
- Concurrent messages process correctly (rapid key presses)

### Key Difference from E2E Tests (bd-1ujr)
E2E tests (bd-1ujr) test the full 68 workflow scenarios at the Message level with
complete behavioral coverage. These integration tests focus on **visual correctness**
of state transitions (rendering output after state changes) and **ftui Model contract**
compliance.

---

**Purpose**: Provide outlier-resistant estimators for process metrics where extreme values (e.g., memory spikes, CPU bursts) could skew Bayesian inference.

**Mathematical Background**:
- M-estimators: Solve Σ ψ((x_i - θ)/σ) = 0 with bounded influence function ψ (Huber, Tukey bisquare)
- Trimmed means: Discard k% extreme values, average remainder - breakdown point = k%
- Median-of-means: Partition into groups, compute medians, take median of medians - sub-Gaussian concentration

**Implementation Requirements**:
1. `robust_mean(values, method)` - Huber, trimmed, median-of-means options
2. `robust_variance(values)` - MAD-based scale estimator: 1.4826 × median(|x - median(x)|)
3. `influence_function(estimator)` - Compute IF for diagnostics
4. Tunable breakdown points and efficiency tradeoffs

**Why This Matters for pt**:
Process metrics have heavy tails (one runaway process shouldn't bias fleet-wide estimates). Robust estimators ensure stable priors even with adversarial data.

**Integration Points**:
- Used by hazard rate estimation (Section 4.4)
- Used by change-point detection (Section 4.6)
- Used by fleet-wide pooling (Section 3.8)

- Verify breakdown point under contamination
- Verify efficiency vs OLS under Gaussian
- Benchmark against numpy/scipy implementations","notes":"Duplicate/out-of-tree bead.  {"id":"process_triage-9ch","title":"Add BATS test job with matrix build","description":"## Purpose
Add a CI job that runs BATS tests on both Linux and macOS to ensure cross-platform compatibility.

## Parent Epic
GitHub Actions CI/CD Pipeline (process_triage-68c)

## Depends On
- Add bash syntax validation job to CI

## Why Matrix Build?
- pt should work on both Linux and macOS
- Some bash features differ between GNU (Linux) and BSD (macOS)
- macOS ships with bash 3.2 by default (we need 4.0+)

## Implementation

### Add to ci.yml
```yaml
  tests:
    name: Tests (${{ matrix.os }})
    runs-on: ${{ matrix.os }}
    strategy:
      fail-fast: false
      matrix:
        os: [ubuntu-latest, macos-latest]
    
    steps:
      - name: Checkout
        uses: actions/checkout@v4
      
      - name: Install Bash 5 (macOS)
        if: runner.os == 'macOS'
        run: |
          brew install bash
          echo "/opt/homebrew/bin" >> $GITHUB_PATH
          # Verify version
          /opt/homebrew/bin/bash --version
      
      - name: Install BATS
        run: |
          git clone --depth 1 https://github.com/bats-core/bats-core.git
          cd bats-core
          sudo ./install.sh /usr/local
      
      - name: Install gum (for integration tests)
        run: |
          if [[ "$RUNNER_OS" == "Linux" ]]; then
            sudo mkdir -p /etc/apt/keyrings
            curl -fsSL https://repo.charm.sh/apt/gpg.key | sudo gpg --dearmor -o /etc/apt/keyrings/charm.gpg
            echo "deb [signed-by=/etc/apt/keyrings/charm.gpg] https://repo.charm.sh/apt/ * *" | sudo tee /etc/apt/sources.list.d/charm.list
            sudo apt update && sudo apt install -y gum
          else
            brew install gum
          fi
        continue-on-error: true  # Tests should work without gum too
      
      - name: Run BATS tests
        run: |
          bats --tap test/
        env:
          TERM: xterm-256color
      
      - name: Upload test results
        if: always()
        uses: actions/upload-artifact@v4
        with:
          name: test-results-${{ matrix.os }}
          path: test/
          retention-days: 14
```

## macOS Bash Version Issue
macOS ships with bash 3.2 (ancient, from 2007) due to licensing.
We need bash 4.0+ for:
- Associative arrays (`declare -A`)
- `mapfile` / `readarray`
- Various modern features

Solution: Install bash 5 via Homebrew on macOS runners.

## Success Criteria
- [ ] Tests run on ubuntu-latest
- [ ] Tests run on macos-latest

 {"id":"process_triage-9jo","title":"Add structured error messages with remediation","description":"## Purpose
Add a consistent, structured error format that includes:
- what failed,
- why (best-effort), and
- what to do next.

This is critical for both humans and agents:
- humans need actionable remediation without reading source
- agents need stable error codes and structured context

## Requirements
### Human-facing
- stderr output includes a short headline + “Reason” + “Fix/Hint”.
- avoid leaking sensitive strings (respect redaction policy).

### Agent-facing
- JSON errors include:
  - stable `code`
  - `category`
  - `recoverable`
  - `suggested_action`
  - structured `context`

### Partial success
Batch operations (e.g., apply many actions) must report partial success cleanly.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Common failure modes have unique stable error codes.
- [ ] Errors include remediation hints when possible.
- [ ] Agent JSON errors are schema-stable and machine-parseable.

## Test Plan
- Unit: error formatting helpers.
- Integration: fixture failures produce expected codes/messages.

-{"id":"process_triage-9k8","title":"EPIC: Phase 16 - Differential and Resumable Sessions","description":"## Overview
Add session resumability and differential scanning so repeated runs surface *what changed* and long-running triage workflows can be paused/resumed safely.

This phase is a key usability multiplier: it reduces repeated work, enables automation, and supports daemon/agent workflows where scans are frequent.

## Background & Rationale
Triage is inherently iterative:
- Users scan, investigate, defer, re-scan later.
- Agents propose a plan, wait for approval, then re-verify.
- Daemons produce periodic snapshots.

Without durable sessions and “since last time” diffs, the system wastes time and users lose context.

## Scope
1. **Differential mode (`--since`)**
   - Persist baseline snapshot per session/run
   - Compute deltas (new/changed/resolved candidates)
   - Efficiently re-scan only changed processes where possible
2. **Resumable sessions**
   - Persist session state to artifact directory
   - `pt agent apply --session <id> --resume` semantics
   - Preserve evidence ledger + decisions + pending actions
3. **Session comparison & reporting**
   - Compare session A vs session B: trend summaries, recurring offenders
   - Support export/report generation for deltas
4. **Safety invariants**
   - Revalidate `(pid,start_id,uid,…)` before any action
   - Stale sessions must fail closed (require re-scan) when identity mismatches

## Data Model
- Snapshot schema for process inventory, evidence summaries, and inference outputs
- Delta schema for changes between snapshots
- Indexing strategy for fast comparisons (hashes keyed by identity tuple)

## UX Requirements
- Default mode should be able to show: “Since last scan: +3 new candidates, 2 resolved, 1 got worse.”
- Agent mode should output machine-readable delta summaries.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Implements snapshot persistence and delta computation with stable schemas.
- [ ] `--since` produces correct new/changed/resolved sets on synthetic fixtures.
- [ ] Resume flow preserves context and blocks execution on identity mismatch.
- [ ] Provides clear user messaging for stale/resume conflicts.
- [ ] Includes E2E tests covering resume + diff workflows.

## Test Plan
- Unit tests: identity hashing, delta classification, session serialization.
- Integration tests: scan → snapshot → mutate process set → diff.
- E2E: agent session pause/resume with verification step.

 {"id":"process_triage-9k8.1","title":"Implement session snapshot persistence (inventory + inference + plan)","description":"## Context
Phase 16. Persist durable session artifacts to enable diff/resume.

## Requirements
- Define snapshot artifacts written to the session artifact directory:
  - process inventory (identity tuples)
  - evidence summaries and/or pointers to telemetry partitions
  - inference outputs (posterior + ledger summary)
  - generated plan (if any)
  - run metadata (host, time, tool versions, config hashes)
- Ensure sensitive strings are redacted/hashed per policy.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Snapshot format is versioned and schema-validated.
- [ ] Snapshot writing is atomic (no partial/corrupt artifacts).
- [ ] Snapshot includes identity protection fields needed for later revalidation.

## Test Plan
- Unit tests: serialization, versioning, atomic write helper.
- Integration: scan→snapshot produces readable artifacts.

 {"id":"process_triage-9k8.2","title":"Implement differential scanning (--since) and delta classification","description":"## Context
Phase 16. Compute changes between snapshots/runs.

## Requirements
- CLI flag: `--since <session-or-snapshot-id>` or `--since <timestamp>`
- Delta classification:
  - new candidates
  - resolved candidates
  - changed candidates (score/posterior drift)
  - worsened/improved categories (optional)
- Efficiency:
  - avoid deep scanning unchanged identities when possible

## Safety
- Never act purely on a delta; always revalidate identity before actions.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Correctly identifies new/changed/resolved sets on fixtures.
- [ ] Outputs a stable delta schema for agent consumption.
- [ ] Efficient path exists for “mostly unchanged” scans.

## Test Plan
- Golden tests: snapshot A/B fixtures.
- Property tests: symmetry (diff(A,A)=empty), idempotence.

 {"id":"process_triage-9k8.3","title":"Implement resumable apply workflow with strict identity revalidation","description":"## Context
Phase 16. Enable pausing and later resuming a plan execution.

## Requirements
- Command: `pt agent apply --session <id> --resume`
- Resume semantics:
  - load prior plan + context
  - re-run verification: identity tuple match, state gates (open write FDs, zombies, D-state)
  - if any mismatch, fail closed and require re-scan/re-plan
- Durable execution log appended during resume.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Resume refuses to run when any target identity mismatches.
- [ ] Resume can continue a partially executed plan idempotently.
- [ ] Post-resume summary includes what was applied before vs after.

## Test Plan
- E2E: create plan, execute first step, stop, resume and complete.
- Negative test: change PID/identity and ensure resume fails closed.
